data.content_ref: inline:The claim's mechanism relies on earthquake storms destroying palatial storage and redistribution infrastructure, yet the scope explicitly excludes Egypt on the basis that its grain storage was decentralized and temple-based. However, the forbidden cases do not include the observation that major Egyptian sites (e.g., Pi-Ramesses, Memphis) also suffered seismic damage in the same period or that Egypt's New Kingdom temple economy was itself a redistributive system vulnerable to storage disruption. The account's survival of Egypt is attributed to a structural difference (temple-based vs. palace-based redistribution) that is asserted rather than defended against the known historical reality that Egyptian temples functioned as redistributive economic centers with significant grain storage. The forbidden cases test the Mycenaean/Hittite side of the mechanism but leave the Egypt-exclusion claim insulated from refutation by seismic evidence affecting Egyptian storage infrastructure.
